narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 36.50, close to the average of 36.70, and your decile rankings from early years of schooling. It also noted your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students in the dataset. For you specifically, the model estimated a 52.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, tipping the scales just enough to recommend rejecting your application to minimize the risk of poor outcomes down the line. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of 5.53 and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, thanks to it being stronger than typical. Your Year 1 decile of 3, below the average of 5.71, also contributed toward the failure side, though with less force than your later-year performance. Compared to typical students who pass, your mid-range LSAT and full-time status were neutral, but those lower deciles stood out as bigger hurdles in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a solid background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on bolstering your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, perhaps by seeking tutoring or advanced classes to demonstrate upward momentum. Consider retaking the LSAT if feasible to push above average, and gather letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to law school.
